title: Angioedema — Acute
oneLiner: Acute swelling of deep dermal/submucosal layers, potentially life-threatening due to laryngeal involvement; distinguish allergic from bradykinin-mediated rapidly.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Upper airway obstruction (laryngeal oedema):** Stridor, hoarse voice, drooling, or inability to swallow — this is NOT predictable by severity of facial swelling. Immediate ABCD approach, call senior airway team + ICU, prepare cricothyroidotomy if worsening despite adrenaline.
- **Anaphylactic shock:** Hypotension, urticaria, wheeze — treat as anaphylaxis first (IM adrenaline, IV fluids) even if you suspect bradykinin-mediated; the wrong guess can be fatal.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset swelling of lips, tongue, eyelids, face, oropharynx, genitals, hands/feet.
  - May include abdominal pain (bowel wall oedema in hereditary angioedema).
  - Pruritus? Usually absent in bradykinin-mediated; present in allergic (urticaria often coexists).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Swelling — non-pitting, symmetrical, well-demarcated.
  - Airway assessment: listen for stridor, check voice change, drooling, oxygen saturations.
  - Skin: check for urticaria (allergic) versus no urticaria (bradykinin).
  - Signs of anaphylaxis: tachycardia, hypotension, wheeze.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Allergic angioedema (histaminergic):** occurs with urticaria, pruritus, anaphylaxis trigger (food, drug, sting). Responds to antihistamines/adrenaline.
- **ACE-inhibitor induced angioedema (bradykinin-mediated):** no urticaria, onset weeks–years after starting ACEi, especially in black ethnicity, common cause of presentations. Does NOT respond to antihistamines/adrenaline.
- **Hereditary angioedema (HAE) (C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency):** family history, recurrent episodes without urticaria, often triggered by trauma/stress/dental work. Abdominal pain common.
- **Acquired angioedema:** associated with lymphoproliferative or autoimmune disorders, low C1q.
- **Scombroid poisoning:** histamine from spoiled fish — facial flushing, headache, diarrhoea — mimics anaphylaxis but resolves quickly with antihistamines.
- **Cellulitis / periorbital cellulitis:** gradual onset, fever, tenderness, warmth, over days — not acute minutes to hours.
- **Splitter:** “Urticaria present + good response to antihistamines → allergic; no urticaria + not responsive to antihistamines/adrenaline → bradykinin-mediated (ACEi, HAE).”
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Airway assessment (Mallampati, voice change, stridor) — do this immediately.
  - Vital signs (NEWS2), pulse oximetry.
  - If anaphylaxis suspected: check for urticaria, wheeze.
- **Bloods:**
  - Full blood count, renal function, liver function, CRP.
  - Baseline C4 level (if bradykinin-mediated suspected; low in HAE and acquired).
  - C1 esterase inhibitor level and function (if family history or recurrent episodes).
  - Consider tryptase level (send urgently if anaphylaxis; must be timed: within 1 hour of onset, repeat at 3h, and a baseline later).
- **Imaging:**
  - None routinely needed for acute presentation.
  - Chest X-ray if stridor or to rule out foreign body.
  - If airway concern, lateral neck X-ray for supraglottic oedema (rarely practical — CT airway may be done by anaesthetics/ENT).
- **Special tests:**
  - C1 inhibitor mutation analysis for genetic confirmation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Airway assessment** — clinical evaluation for impending obstruction must be done before any investigation. Document the patency of the airway and the plan.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABCDE approach:** high-flow O₂, IV access, monitor, call senior airway/anaesthetics if stridor or voice change.
  - **If anaphylaxis (with urticaria, hypotension, wheeze):** IM adrenaline 500 micrograms (0.5 mL of 1:1000) into anterolateral thigh, repeat every 5 min if no improvement; IV fluids (crystalloid); chlorphenamine 10 mg IV; hydrocortisone 200 mg IV.
  - **If bradykinin-mediated suspected (no urticaria, on ACEi or known HAE):** Treat with icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist) subcutaneous 30 mg; or C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate (Berinert) IV; or ecallantide (UK restricted, often not first line). **Fresh frozen plasma (FFP)** may be used if specific agents unavailable (risks: infection, fluid overload). Stop ACEi immediately and counsel patient never to restart.
  - **If uncertain:** treat as anaphylaxis first (adrenaline is safe even in bradykinin-mediated if severe) — then reassess. Do not delay airway management.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor in high-dependency area/ICU if airway involved.
  - Continue antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg daily) for allergic angioedema.
  - HAE patients: consider prophylaxis with danazol or C1 inhibitor concentrate for upcoming procedures.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Allergic: identify trigger, refer to allergy clinic, provide adrenaline auto-injector and training.
  - ACEi-induced: switch to ARB (but cross-reaction possible, though rare). ARB can cause angioedema in about 0.1-0.2% (vs 0.1-0.7% for ACEi). Alternative antihypertensives: calcium channel blockers, thiazides, beta-blockers.
  - HAE: refer to immunology, consider long-term prophylaxis (danazol, stanozolol, tranexamic acid, or C1 inhibitor concentrate).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - NBM until airway safe and reviewed by senior.
  - IV fluids: crystalloid at maintenance rate (e.g. 1 litre 0.9% NaCl over 8 hours) only if nil by mouth or hypotensive.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (if no bleeding risk) — but use cautiously in HAE because trauma can trigger attacks (avoid if possible).
  - Observations: minimum hourly NEWS2; if airway concern, continuous SpO₂ + call anaesthetics.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Adrenaline (epinephrine):** 0.5 mg IM (0.5 mL of 1:1000) — repeat q5min as needed. Dose for children: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) IM.
  - **Chlorphenamine:** 10 mg IV (slow over 1 min) — for allergic angioedema.
  - **Hydrocortisone:** 200 mg IV (slow bolus) — for allergic.
  - **Icatibant:** 30 mg subcutaneous into abdomen (1 vial). May repeat after 6 hours if needed. BNF entry: max 3 doses in 24 hours.
  - **C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate (Berinert):** IV infusion over 5-10 min. Adult dose: 20 IU/kg ([check local policy] as vials vary; usually 1000-2000 IU).
  - **FFP:** 2-4 units IV if no specific agents available (risk: volume overload).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Ecallantide:** 30 mg subcutaneous — only via specialist recommendation (restricted).
  - **Cetirizine:** 10 mg PO/V once daily for maintenance.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Adrenaline: caution in ischaemic heart disease or hypertension (but still give in anaphylaxis).
  - ACEi: absolutely contraindicated in any history of angioedema (allergic or bradykinin).
  - Danazol: teratogenic; hepatotoxic; use in females requires contraception.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Most drugs used acutely (adrenaline, chlorphenamine, hydrocortisone) are single doses — no significant adjustment needed.
  - Icatibant: no dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal/hepatic impairment. For end-stage renal disease: limited data; use with caution.
  - C1 inhibitor concentrate: no adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IM adrenaline 1:1000 IV** — this causes cardiac arrest. Always check concentration: IM = 1:1000 (1 mg/mL), IV = 1:10000 (0.1 mg/mL). **Also: using antihistamines alone in bradykinin-mediated angioedema** — they are ineffective and delay proper treatment.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Complete airway obstruction requiring emergency intubation or cricothyroidotomy.
  - Hypoxic brain injury from airway compromise.
  - Aspiration if severe oropharyngeal oedema.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent episodes (HAE) — quality of life impacted, risk of asphyxiation with each attack.
  - ACEi-induced: if not stopped, risk of fatal laryngeal oedema later.
  - Psychological distress (fear of recurrence).
sections.Prognosis:
- Allergic angioedema: excellent if treated promptly; death <0.1% of anaphylaxis cases.
- ACEi-induced: risk of recurrence if drug not stopped; once stopped, attacks usually resolve over 24-72h.
- HAE: lifelong condition; with appropriate prophylaxis and acute treatment, mortality can approach zero. Without treatment, historical mortality ~30% due to asphyxiation.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **In every patient with unexplained or recurrent angioedema, check a C4 level and C1 inhibitor function.** Low C4 is a cheap, rapid screen for HAE (high sensitivity).
- **Document clearly if the patient is on ACEi** and immediately stop the drug and advise alternative antihypertensive.
- **Always have an airway tray and call senior anaesthetist early** if stridor or voice change — do not wait for SpO₂ to drop.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give antihistamines alone if stridor present** — adrenaline is the only airway-saving drug.
- **Do not discharge a patient with ACEi-induced angioedema without stopping the ACEi** and documenting this in the record and to GP.
- **Do not assume every angioedema is allergic** — consider bradykinin-mediated if no urticaria, especially in black patients or those on ACEi; wrong treatment delays recovery.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening of throat tightness, voice change, drooling, difficulty swallowing.
  - Any new swelling of face, lips, or tongue.
  - New urticaria or difficulty breathing.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Allergic: refer to allergy clinic within 4 weeks; ensure patient has adrenaline auto-injector and knows how to use it.
  - ACEi-induced: follow up with GP within 1 week to adjust antihypertensives.
  - Possible HAE: refer to immunology for confirmatory testing.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) for:**
  - Any stridor, voice change, or drooling.
  - Failure to improve 30 minutes after first-line treatment.
  - Suspected HAE or ACEi-induced with no improvement after icatibant/FFP.
- **Call ICU/crash team for:**
  - Stridor with respiratory distress (sats <92%, use of accessory muscles).
  - Hypotension unresponsive to initial adrenaline and fluids.
  - GCS <15 or seizure.
  - NEWS2 >= 7 or any single red parameter (respiratory rate >25, SpO2 <90%, systolic BP <90 mmHg).

rawMarkdown:
## Angioedema — Acute
**One-liner:** Acute swelling of deep dermal/submucosal layers, potentially life-threatening due to laryngeal involvement; distinguish allergic from bradykinin-mediated rapidly.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Upper airway obstruction (laryngeal oedema):** Stridor, hoarse voice, drooling, or inability to swallow — this is NOT predictable by severity of facial swelling. Immediate ABCD approach, call senior airway team + ICU, prepare cricothyroidotomy if worsening despite adrenaline.
- **Anaphylactic shock:** Hypotension, urticaria, wheeze — treat as anaphylaxis first (IM adrenaline, IV fluids) even if you suspect bradykinin-mediated; the wrong guess can be fatal.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Sudden onset swelling of lips, tongue, eyelids, face, oropharynx, genitals, hands/feet.
  - May include abdominal pain (bowel wall oedema in hereditary angioedema).
  - Pruritus? Usually absent in bradykinin-mediated; present in allergic (urticaria often coexists).
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Swelling — non-pitting, symmetrical, well-demarcated.
  - Airway assessment: listen for stridor, check voice change, drooling, oxygen saturations.
  - Skin: check for urticaria (allergic) versus no urticaria (bradykinin).
  - Signs of anaphylaxis: tachycardia, hypotension, wheeze.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Allergic angioedema (histaminergic):** occurs with urticaria, pruritus, anaphylaxis trigger (food, drug, sting). Responds to antihistamines/adrenaline.
- **ACE-inhibitor induced angioedema (bradykinin-mediated):** no urticaria, onset weeks–years after starting ACEi, especially in black ethnicity, common cause of presentations. Does NOT respond to antihistamines/adrenaline.
- **Hereditary angioedema (HAE) (C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency):** family history, recurrent episodes without urticaria, often triggered by trauma/stress/dental work. Abdominal pain common.
- **Acquired angioedema:** associated with lymphoproliferative or autoimmune disorders, low C1q.
- **Scombroid poisoning:** histamine from spoiled fish — facial flushing, headache, diarrhoea — mimics anaphylaxis but resolves quickly with antihistamines.
- **Cellulitis / periorbital cellulitis:** gradual onset, fever, tenderness, warmth, over days — not acute minutes to hours.
- **Splitter:** “Urticaria present + good response to antihistamines → allergic; no urticaria + not responsive to antihistamines/adrenaline → bradykinin-mediated (ACEi, HAE).”

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Airway assessment (Mallampati, voice change, stridor) — do this immediately.
  - Vital signs (NEWS2), pulse oximetry.
  - If anaphylaxis suspected: check for urticaria, wheeze.
- **Bloods:**
  - Full blood count, renal function, liver function, CRP.
  - Baseline C4 level (if bradykinin-mediated suspected; low in HAE and acquired).
  - C1 esterase inhibitor level and function (if family history or recurrent episodes).
  - Consider tryptase level (send urgently if anaphylaxis; must be timed: within 1 hour of onset, repeat at 3h, and a baseline later).
- **Imaging:**
  - None routinely needed for acute presentation.
  - Chest X-ray if stridor or to rule out foreign body.
  - If airway concern, lateral neck X-ray for supraglottic oedema (rarely practical — CT airway may be done by anaesthetics/ENT).
- **Special tests:**
  - C1 inhibitor mutation analysis for genetic confirmation.
- **Never-miss test:** **Airway assessment** — clinical evaluation for impending obstruction must be done before any investigation. Document the patency of the airway and the plan.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **ABCDE approach:** high-flow O₂, IV access, monitor, call senior airway/anaesthetics if stridor or voice change.
  - **If anaphylaxis (with urticaria, hypotension, wheeze):** IM adrenaline 500 micrograms (0.5 mL of 1:1000) into anterolateral thigh, repeat every 5 min if no improvement; IV fluids (crystalloid); chlorphenamine 10 mg IV; hydrocortisone 200 mg IV.
  - **If bradykinin-mediated suspected (no urticaria, on ACEi or known HAE):** Treat with icatibant (bradykinin B2 receptor antagonist) subcutaneous 30 mg; or C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate (Berinert) IV; or ecallantide (UK restricted, often not first line). **Fresh frozen plasma (FFP)** may be used if specific agents unavailable (risks: infection, fluid overload). Stop ACEi immediately and counsel patient never to restart.
  - **If uncertain:** treat as anaphylaxis first (adrenaline is safe even in bradykinin-mediated if severe) — then reassess. Do not delay airway management.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor in high-dependency area/ICU if airway involved.
  - Continue antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg daily) for allergic angioedema.
  - HAE patients: consider prophylaxis with danazol or C1 inhibitor concentrate for upcoming procedures.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Allergic: identify trigger, refer to allergy clinic, provide adrenaline auto-injector and training.
  - ACEi-induced: switch to ARB (but cross-reaction possible, though rare). ARB can cause angioedema in about 0.1-0.2% (vs 0.1-0.7% for ACEi). Alternative antihypertensives: calcium channel blockers, thiazides, beta-blockers.
  - HAE: refer to immunology, consider long-term prophylaxis (danazol, stanozolol, tranexamic acid, or C1 inhibitor concentrate).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - NBM until airway safe and reviewed by senior.
  - IV fluids: crystalloid at maintenance rate (e.g. 1 litre 0.9% NaCl over 8 hours) only if nil by mouth or hypotensive.
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin (if no bleeding risk) — but use cautiously in HAE because trauma can trigger attacks (avoid if possible).
  - Observations: minimum hourly NEWS2; if airway concern, continuous SpO₂ + call anaesthetics.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Adrenaline (epinephrine):** 0.5 mg IM (0.5 mL of 1:1000) — repeat q5min as needed. Dose for children: 0.01 mg/kg (max 0.5 mg) IM.
  - **Chlorphenamine:** 10 mg IV (slow over 1 min) — for allergic angioedema.
  - **Hydrocortisone:** 200 mg IV (slow bolus) — for allergic.
  - **Icatibant:** 30 mg subcutaneous into abdomen (1 vial). May repeat after 6 hours if needed. BNF entry: max 3 doses in 24 hours.
  - **C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate (Berinert):** IV infusion over 5-10 min. Adult dose: 20 IU/kg ([check local policy] as vials vary; usually 1000-2000 IU).
  - **FFP:** 2-4 units IV if no specific agents available (risk: volume overload).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Ecallantide:** 30 mg subcutaneous — only via specialist recommendation (restricted).
  - **Cetirizine:** 10 mg PO/V once daily for maintenance.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Adrenaline: caution in ischaemic heart disease or hypertension (but still give in anaphylaxis).
  - ACEi: absolutely contraindicated in any history of angioedema (allergic or bradykinin).
  - Danazol: teratogenic; hepatotoxic; use in females requires contraception.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Most drugs used acutely (adrenaline, chlorphenamine, hydrocortisone) are single doses — no significant adjustment needed.
  - Icatibant: no dose adjustment needed for mild-moderate renal/hepatic impairment. For end-stage renal disease: limited data; use with caution.
  - C1 inhibitor concentrate: no adjustment needed.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving IM adrenaline 1:1000 IV** — this causes cardiac arrest. Always check concentration: IM = 1:1000 (1 mg/mL), IV = 1:10000 (0.1 mg/mL). **Also: using antihistamines alone in bradykinin-mediated angioedema** — they are ineffective and delay proper treatment.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Complete airway obstruction requiring emergency intubation or cricothyroidotomy.
  - Hypoxic brain injury from airway compromise.
  - Aspiration if severe oropharyngeal oedema.
- **Chronic:**
  - Recurrent episodes (HAE) — quality of life impacted, risk of asphyxiation with each attack.
  - ACEi-induced: if not stopped, risk of fatal laryngeal oedema later.
  - Psychological distress (fear of recurrence).

### Prognosis
- Allergic angioedema: excellent if treated promptly; death <0.1% of anaphylaxis cases.
- ACEi-induced: risk of recurrence if drug not stopped; once stopped, attacks usually resolve over 24-72h.
- HAE: lifelong condition; with appropriate prophylaxis and acute treatment, mortality can approach zero. Without treatment, historical mortality ~30% due to asphyxiation.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **In every patient with unexplained or recurrent angioedema, check a C4 level and C1 inhibitor function.** Low C4 is a cheap, rapid screen for HAE (high sensitivity).
- **Document clearly if the patient is on ACEi** and immediately stop the drug and advise alternative antihypertensive.
- **Always have an airway tray and call senior anaesthetist early** if stridor or voice change — do not wait for SpO₂ to drop.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give antihistamines alone if stridor present** — adrenaline is the only airway-saving drug.
- **Do not discharge a patient with ACEi-induced angioedema without stopping the ACEi** and documenting this in the record and to GP.
- **Do not assume every angioedema is allergic** — consider bradykinin-mediated if no urticaria, especially in black patients or those on ACEi; wrong treatment delays recovery.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening of throat tightness, voice change, drooling, difficulty swallowing.
  - Any new swelling of face, lips, or tongue.
  - New urticaria or difficulty breathing.
- **Follow-up:**
  - Allergic: refer to allergy clinic within 4 weeks; ensure patient has adrenaline auto-injector and knows how to use it.
  - ACEi-induced: follow up with GP within 1 week to adjust antihypertensives.
  - Possible HAE: refer to immunology for confirmatory testing.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) for:**
  - Any stridor, voice change, or drooling.
  - Failure to improve 30 minutes after first-line treatment.
  - Suspected HAE or ACEi-induced with no improvement after icatibant/FFP.
- **Call ICU/crash team for:**
  - Stridor with respiratory distress (sats <92%, use of accessory muscles).
  - Hypotension unresponsive to initial adrenaline and fluids.
  - GCS <15 or seizure.
  - NEWS2 >= 7 or any single red parameter (respiratory rate >25, SpO2 <90%, systolic BP <90 mmHg).
